source_paper_title: Rates of Convergence in the Central Limit Theorem for Markov Chains, with an Application to TD Learning
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Srikant_2025, title={Rates of Convergence in the Central Limit Theorem for Markov Chains, with an Application to TD Learning}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0444}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0444}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Srikant, R.}, year={2025}, month=oct }
context:
Section 5 (Conclusions), page 19

Under some conditions, the rate of convergence in the central limit theorem for i.i.d. random vectors is \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) (Fang et al. 2019). It would be interesting to see if the \(O(\log n/\sqrt n)\) rate of convergence in Theorem 1 can be improved to \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) under possibly additional conditions than the ones used in this paper.
title: Improve Wasserstein convergence rate for vector martingale central limit theorem
background_latex:
Let \(\{m_k\}_{k\ge 1}\) be a \(d\)-dimensional martingale difference sequence with respect to a filtration \(\{\mathcal F_k\}_{k\ge 0}\), meaning \(m_k\) is \(\mathcal F_k\)-measurable, \(\mathbb E\|m_k\|<\infty\), and \(\mathbb E[m_k\mid \mathcal F_{k-1}]=0\) for all \(k\ge 1\). Define partial sums \(S_n=\sum_{k=1}^n m_k\) and the normalized sum \(W_n=S_n/\sqrt n\).

Assume there exists \(\beta\in(0,1)\) such that \(\mathbb E\|m_k\|^{2+\beta}<\infty\) for all \(k\), and the conditional covariance matrices \(\Sigma_k:=\mathbb E[m_k m_k^\top\mid \mathcal F_{k-1}]\) exist. Let \(\Sigma_\infty\succ 0\) be a positive definite matrix intended to represent the limiting (asymptotic) covariance of \(W_n\) in a martingale central limit theorem.

Let \(Z\sim\mathcal N(0,I_d)\). For random vectors \(X,Y\in\mathbb R^d\), the \(1\)-Wasserstein distance is
\[
 d_W(X,Y)=\sup_{h\in\mathrm{Lip}_1} \mathbb E[h(X)-h(Y)],
\]
where \(\mathrm{Lip}_1\) is the set of real-valued functions on \(\mathbb R^d\) with Lipschitz constant at most 1 w.r.t. the Euclidean norm.

A non-asymptotic martingale CLT bound in Wasserstein distance can have the form
\[
 d_W\bigl(W_n,\, \Sigma_\infty^{1/2} Z\bigr) \le \text{(explicit bound depending on moments, }\Sigma_k,\Sigma_\infty\text{)}.
\]
In the paper, a general bound is proved that (in typical applications) yields the rate \(O(\log n/\sqrt n)\) in \(d_W\).
problem_definition_latex:
Find additional, verifiable conditions on the martingale difference sequence \(\{m_k\}\) (beyond the finite-\((2+\beta)\) moment and conditional covariance assumptions stated above) under which the Wasserstein error
\[
 d_W\bigl(W_n,\, \Sigma_\infty^{1/2} Z\bigr)
\]
admits an improved rate of convergence \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) (i.e., with the \(\log n\) factor removed) as \(n\to\infty\), while keeping a vector-valued martingale setting and a Gaussian limit with covariance \(\Sigma_\infty\).
latex:

open_problem_quote: Under some conditions, the rate of convergence in the central limit theorem for i.i.d. random vectors is \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) (Fang et al. 2019). It would be interesting to see if the \(O(\log n/\sqrt n)\) rate of convergence in Theorem 1 can be improved to \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) under possibly additional conditions than the ones used in this paper.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can computationally search for martingale difference arrays where the Wasserstein distance decays like \(c\log n/\sqrt n\) or \(c/\sqrt n\) by simulating candidate constructions and estimating \(d_W\) numerically, but accurate Wasserstein estimation in moderate dimension is nontrivial and noisy, making automated counterexample discovery only moderately feasible.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate sufficient conditions or refined bounds can be explored by generating parametric families of martingales (e.g., functions of Markov chains via Poisson equations) and numerically regressing the decay of \(d_W\) as a fitness proxy; however, the search space over "conditions" is not directly codable, and the main bottleneck is computing a reliable Wasserstein-distance proxy, so suitability is moderate rather than high.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
Srikant’s 2026 paper proves a non-asymptotic vector martingale CLT in 1-Wasserstein distance via a Lindeberg decomposition combined with multivariate Stein regularity estimates (Gallou\"et–Mijoule–Swan; Fang–Shao–Xu). The resulting bound (Theorem 1) has a typical \(O((\log n)/\sqrt n)\) rate when one pushes the Hölder parameter \(\beta\uparrow 1\) to approach the i.i.d.-optimal \(n^{-1/2}\) scaling; the \(\log n\) arises from the blow-up of Stein-solution Hölder constants like \((1-\beta)^{-1}\). In the Markov-chain application, Poisson’s equation yields a martingale difference sequence with bounded moments and geometrically decaying covariance mismatch \(\|\mathbb E\Sigma_k-\Sigma_\infty\|\), but the same Stein-regularity mechanism still produces \(O((\log n)/\sqrt n)\) in Wasserstein-1.

Among forward citations, the most direct evidence that the \(\log n\) factor is not intrinsic comes from “Wasserstein-p Central Limit Theorem Rates: From Local Dependence to Markov Chains”, which attains the optimal \(O(n^{-1/2})\) Wasserstein-1 rate for additive functionals of geometrically ergodic Markov chains under drift/minorization and \((2+\delta)\)-moment domination (with \(\delta>1\)), and in the homogeneous case transfers this to \(\mathcal N(0,\Sigma_\infty)\) when the covariance stabilizes at \(O(1/n)\). This suggests that log-free \(n^{-1/2}\) rates are achievable in dependent settings when one avoids the particular Stein-equation regularity step that forces \(\beta\uparrow 1\), for example via local-dependence/regeneration couplings or alternative Stein couplings tailored to Markov chains.

However, none of the citing works provides a general theorem for arbitrary \(d\)-dimensional martingale differences \(m_k\) in terms of predictable covariances \(\Sigma_k=\mathbb E[m_km_k^\top\mid\mathcal F_{k-1}]\) that removes the \(\log n\) while keeping only verifiable structural assumptions. Several papers in stochastic approximation and RL (two-time-scale SA, Q-learning, nonlinear SA) explicitly rely on martingale Wasserstein CLTs with \(\log n\) losses and identify improved martingale Wasserstein bounds as a key missing ingredient. Overall, the problem remains open at the level of a general vector martingale CLT, though optimal \(O(n^{-1/2})\) rates are known in important subclasses (notably geometrically ergodic Markov-chain additive functionals) under stronger dependence/moment and covariance-stabilization conditions.

sources.title: Wasserstein-p Central Limit Theorem Rates: From Local Dependence to Markov Chains
sources.theorem_or_result:
For additive functionals of a geometrically ergodic Markov chain 
\[S_n=\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} h_i(x_i),\qquad \Sigma_n:=\tfrac1n\mathrm{Var}(S_n),\]
under a drift/minorization (geometric ergodicity) condition and a moment domination \(\|h_i\|_{2+\delta}\le V\) for some \(\delta>1\), the paper proves an optimal Wasserstein-1 CLT rate
\[W_1\bigl(\mathcal L(S_n/\sqrt n),\,\mathcal N(0,\Sigma_n)\bigr)=O(n^{-1/2}).\]
In the homogeneous case \(h_i\equiv h\), with \(\Sigma_\infty=\lim_{n\to\infty}\Sigma_n\) and \(\|\Sigma_n-\Sigma_\infty\|=O(1/n)\), this yields
\[W_1\bigl(\mathcal L(S_n/\sqrt n),\,\mathcal N(0,\Sigma_\infty)\bigr)=O(n^{-1/2}).\]
(Their Theorem 3 plus a Gaussian covariance-mismatch bound.)
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for additional verifiable conditions on a vector-valued martingale difference array \(m_k\) ensuring an improved Wasserstein-1 martingale CLT rate \(O(n^{-1/2})\) (removing a \(\log n\) factor) for \(W_n=n^{-1/2}\sum_{k=1}^n m_k\) versus \(\mathcal N(0,\Sigma_\infty)\).

This paper does not treat general martingale difference sequences directly, nor does it give a general martingale CLT bound in terms of conditional covariances \(\Sigma_k=\mathbb E[m_km_k^\top\mid\mathcal F_{k-1}]\). Instead it proves an \(O(n^{-1/2})\) \(W_1\) CLT rate for dependent partial sums arising from geometrically ergodic Markov chains (and also for locally dependent sequences), by bypassing Stein-equation regularity estimates that cause \(\log n\) losses.

Nevertheless, it provides concrete *additional conditions* (geometric ergodicity + a Lyapunov domination \(\|h_i\|_{2+\delta}\le V\) with \(\delta>1\), plus nondegenerate covariance) under which one gets the desired \(O(n^{-1/2})\) Wasserstein-1 rate for a broad dependent-data class. Via standard martingale decompositions of additive functionals, their techniques are conceptually relevant to martingale CLTs, but they do not resolve the open problem in the full generality of arbitrary vector martingale differences with only moment and conditional covariance assumptions.

sources.title: Nonasymptotic clt and error bounds for two-time-scale stochastic approximation
sources.theorem_or_result:
For linear two-time-scale stochastic approximation with Polyak–Ruppert averaging driven by martingale-difference noise \,\(N_t=(W_t,V_t)\) with bounded \((2+\beta)\)-moments and constant conditional covariance \(\mathbb E[N_tN_t^\top\mid H_{t-1}]=\Gamma\), and step sizes \(\alpha_t=\alpha_1 t^{-a},\ \gamma_t=\gamma_1 t^{-b}\) with \(1/2<a<b<1\), the paper proves a non-asymptotic Wasserstein-1 CLT for the scaled averaged errors. In particular (Theorem 1), for \((a,b)\) in a specified region \(\Theta\),
\[
 d_1\bigl(\sqrt n\,G(\bar x_n-x^*),\ \pi_{\bar x}\bigr),\ d_1\bigl(\sqrt n\,\Delta(\bar y_n-y^*),\ \pi_{\bar y}\bigr)
 = O\Bigl(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt n}\bigl(n^{a-1/2}/(a-1/2)+1/(b-a)+n^{b/2}+n^{2a-b-1/2}/(2a-b-1/2)\bigr)\Bigr),
\]
where \(\pi_{\bar x},\pi_{\bar y}\) are centered Gaussians with covariances characterized by limits of \(n^{-1}\operatorname{Cov}(\sum_{t\le n}(W_t-A_{fs}A_{ss}^{-1}V_t))\) and \(n^{-1}\operatorname{Cov}(\sum_{t\le n}(V_t-A_{sf}A_{ff}^{-1}W_t))\). Corollary 1 further yields \(\mathbb E\|\bar x_n-x^*\|,\mathbb E\|\bar y_n-y^*\| = (1/\sqrt n)\,\mathbb E\|Z\| + o(n^{-1/2})\) for the corresponding Gaussian \(Z\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for verifiable conditions on a vector martingale difference array \(m_k\) under which the Wasserstein-1 CLT error for \(W_n=S_n/\sqrt n\) improves from \(O((\log n)/\sqrt n)\) to \(O(1/\sqrt n)\). This citing paper is directly about non-asymptotic Wasserstein-1 CLTs for martingale-driven recursions and explicitly uses a martingale Wasserstein-1 CLT from prior work ([19] in their bibliography). However, it does not provide a general martingale CLT with \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) for \(S_n/\sqrt n\); instead it derives a problem-specific bound for Polyak–Ruppert averaged two-time-scale SA, where the Wasserstein-1 distance to a Gaussian limit decays at rates that are generally slower than \(1/\sqrt n\) (e.g. optimized to about \(O(n^{-1/4})\) for their step-size choices). The paper does, nonetheless, highlight additional structural assumptions beyond mere \((2+\beta)\)-moments—most notably: (i) the noise is a martingale difference sequence independent of the iterates, (ii) the conditional covariance is constant \(\Gamma\) (no time variation), and (iii) linear dynamics allow explicit control of remainder terms. These are the kinds of extra conditions that, in principle, can remove logarithmic factors in martingale CLT bounds, but the paper does not establish the desired \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) Wasserstein rate for the normalized martingale sum itself.

sources.title: Finite-Sample Wasserstein Error Bounds and Concentration Inequalities for Nonlinear Stochastic Approximation
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper develops finite-sample Wasserstein bounds for stochastic approximation by assuming a quantitative martingale/Markov-noise CLT for the driving noise. In particular, for a martingale difference sequence \(\{M_k\}\) satisfying a non-asymptotic \(W_1\)-CLT with third moments (Lemma 2, based on [Srikant 2025]), it obtains for the normalized last iterate \(y_n=\gamma_n^{-1/2}(x_n-x^*)\) the rate
- \(W_1(y_n,U_\infty)=O\bigl(\gamma_n^{1/6}(\log \gamma_n)^{1/3}+E_n^a/\gamma_n\bigr)\) (Theorem 1(1)),
and for Polyak–Ruppert averaging \(\bar y_n=\sqrt n(\bar x_n-x^*)\) a bound reducing to \(W_p(\bar y_n,\bar\Sigma^{1/2}Z)=O(n^{-1/6})\) when \(\gamma_n\asymp n^{-2/3}\) and the martingale sum \(n^{-1/2}\sum_{k=1}^n M_k\) satisfies a \(W_p\)-CLT (Theorem 2).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for additional conditions on a vector-valued martingale difference array \(m_k\) under which the Wasserstein-1 martingale CLT error improves from the typical \(O(\log n/\sqrt n)\) to \(O(1/\sqrt n)\). This paper does not remove the \(\log\) factor; instead it explicitly uses an existing \(W_1\) martingale CLT bound that contains \(d\log n/\sqrt n\) (Lemma 2) and notes that sharper CLTs (e.g., for independent noise or under Poincaré/spectral gap assumptions) could be plugged in. Section 5.1 highlights as an open direction the lack of quantitative martingale CLTs in \(W_p\) for \(p\ge2\), reinforcing that improved martingale Wasserstein rates are not obtained here. Thus, it provides a framework that would immediately yield \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) for SA iterates if one had an \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) martingale \(W_1\)-CLT for \(\sum m_k\), but it does not itself establish new verifiable martingale conditions achieving that improvement.

sources.title: Quantifying Normality: Convergence Rate to Gaussian Limit for Stochastic Approximation and Unadjusted OU Algorithm
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves non-asymptotic Wasserstein-1 bounds for rescaled stochastic approximation (SA) iterates and for a related discrete Ornstein–Uhlenbeck recursion (DOUG). In particular, for DOUG
\[z_{k+1}=(I+\alpha_k J_k^{(\alpha,\xi)})z_k+\sqrt{\alpha_k}\,b(w_k),\]
with i.i.d. mean-zero noise having finite third moment, Theorem 4.2 gives
- for \(\alpha_k=\alpha/(k+K)^\xi\), \(\xi\in(0,1)\): \(d_W(z_k,\Sigma_1^{1/2}Z)\le O\bigl(\tfrac{\log(k+K)}{(k+K)^{\xi/2}}\bigr)+O((k+K)^{-\xi})+\text{transient}\);
- for \(\xi=1\): \(d_W(z_k,\Sigma_2^{1/2}Z)\le O\bigl(\tfrac{\log(k+K)}{\sqrt{k+K}}\bigr)+O((k+K)^{-1})+\text{transient}\).
For SA, Theorem 4.1 yields analogous bounds for \(y_k=(x_k-x^*)/\sqrt{\alpha_k}\), with an additional bias term \(O((k+K)^{-\xi\delta/2})\) from nonlinear drift; when \(\delta=1\) the DOUG term \(O(\log(k)/\sqrt{k})\) is rate-limiting.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for verifiable conditions on a vector martingale difference array \(m_k\) under which the Wasserstein-1 CLT error for \(W_n=n^{-1/2}\sum_{k\le n} m_k\) improves from \(O(\log n/\sqrt n)\) to \(O(1/\sqrt n)\). This paper studies a different but closely related setting: weighted sums of i.i.d. noise (DOUG) and SA iterates, and it explicitly identifies the \(\log\) factor as coming from their Stein-method bound for weighted sums with only a finite third moment assumption. They do not remove the \(\log\) factor; in fact they state improving DOUG’s rate (and hence removing the \(\log\)) as an open question. However, the analysis clarifies which additional structure might help: (i) Gaussian noise yields \(O(\alpha_k)\) rates in related Langevin/ULA work; (ii) symmetric negative definite drift allows sharper norm control; (iii) stronger smoothness/regularity of Stein solutions or stronger moment/tail assumptions could potentially eliminate the \(\log\). Thus the paper provides partial progress/tools but not the desired \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) martingale CLT rate.

sources.title: Central Limit Theorems for Asynchronous Averaged Q-Learning
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves a non-asymptotic 1-Wasserstein CLT for Polyak–Ruppert averaged asynchronous Q-learning iterates. In particular, for stepsizes \(\alpha_k=\alpha(k+b)^{-\beta}\) with \(\beta\in(1/2,1)\), under Assumptions 1–2 it establishes
\[
W_1\Bigl(K^{-1/2}\sum_{k=1}^K \Delta_k,\; \widetilde N\Bigr) \le \frac{\sqrt{|S||A|}}{\rho(1-\gamma)^2\sqrt K}\,\widetilde O\Bigl((\rho(1-\gamma))^{\frac{\beta-2}{1-\beta}} + K^{\beta/2}\rho^{-1}(1-\gamma)^{-1} + K^{1-\beta} + K^{(1-\beta)/2}\rho^{-1-\beta}(1-\gamma)^{-\beta}\Bigr),
\]
where \(\Delta_k=Q_k-Q^*\), \(\widetilde N=(A^{-1}\Sigma A^{-\top})^{1/2}N(0,I)\), \(A=D-\gamma D P_{\pi^*}\), and \(\Sigma\) is defined via the Poisson equation for the underlying finite-state Markov chain. For \(\beta=2/3\) this simplifies to \(W_1( K^{-1/2}\sum_{k=1}^K\Delta_k,\widetilde N)\le \widetilde O(\sqrt{|S||A|}\,K^{-1/6}\rho^{-2}(1-\gamma)^{-3})\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for additional conditions under which a vector martingale CLT in \(W_1\) improves from a typical \(O(\log n/\sqrt n)\) rate to \(O(1/\sqrt n)\). This paper does not remove a \(\log n\) factor in a general martingale CLT; instead it applies an existing non-asymptotic martingale CLT bound (Srikant 2024) to a specific martingale difference sequence obtained from Markovian noise via a Poisson-equation decomposition. The resulting overall \(W_1\) rate for the averaged Q-learning error is slower than \(1/\sqrt K\) (e.g. \(K^{-1/6}\) in the displayed corollary), and some intermediate bounds still involve harmonic sums (\(\sum 1/i\)) that are controlled by \(\widetilde O(\log K)\). Nevertheless, the paper is relevant because it identifies a concrete martingale difference sequence arising from Markovian noise, verifies the moment/covariance conditions needed for the martingale CLT, and shows how Poisson-equation/mixing assumptions yield explicit non-asymptotic \(W_1\) bounds—suggesting that stronger mixing/uniformity conditions might be leveraged to sharpen the summation terms that produce logarithms in general bounds.

sources.title: Gaussian Approximation for Asynchronous Q-learning
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves a high-dimensional Gaussian approximation for sums of -dimensional martingale differences over hyper-rectangles. In particular, under (i) finite third moments \(\mathbb E\|X_k\|_\infty^3<\infty\) and (ii) (in Theorem 1) almost surely deterministic predictable quadratic variation \(\sum_{k=1}^n V_k=\Sigma_n\) with \(V_k=\mathbb E[X_kX_k^\top\mid\mathcal F_{k-1}]\), it bounds the Kolmogorov distance
- \(d_K(S_n,\,\mathcal N(0,\Sigma))\)
by a term of order \(\log^{5/4}(d)\,\sigma(\Sigma_n)^{-1/2}\bigl(\sigma(\Sigma)+\sum_{k=1}^n \mathbb E[\|X_k\|_\infty^3/\lambda_{\min}(P_k+\Sigma)]\bigr)^{1/2}\), where \(P_k=\sum_{i=k}^n V_i\).
It also extends to random predictable variation (Theorem 6) assuming a concentration inequality for \(\sum_{k=1}^n \mathbb E[X_kX_k^\top\mid\mathcal F_{k-1}]\) around \(\Sigma_n\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for conditions yielding an \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) Wasserstein rate for vector martingale CLTs (removing a \(\log n\) factor). This paper does not provide Wasserstein \(d_W\) bounds; instead it gives high-dimensional Kolmogorov bounds over hyper-rectangles for martingale sums, with rates typically \(n^{-1/4}\) (plus polylog factors) and with a key term involving \(\sum_k \lambda_{\min}(P_k+\Sigma)^{-1}\) that is controlled only up to \(n\log n\), producing logarithmic factors. Thus it does not resolve the Wasserstein \(O(1/\sqrt n)\) question, but it develops Stein+Lindeberg techniques and identifies that improving rates would require stronger lower bounds on \(\lambda_{\min}(P_k)\) (or related non-degeneracy/uniform variance growth), which is conceptually related to removing log factors in martingale CLTs.
